MIMIC-IV: A Freely Accessible Electronic Health Record Dataset
  • Source: Scientific Data, Vol. 10:1 (2023) | https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-022-01899-x
  • Topic: Publicly available critical care database from electronic health records
  • Author(s): Johnson A.E.W., Bulgarelli L., Shen L., Gayles A., Shammout A., Horng S., Pollard T.J., Hao S., Moody B., Gow B., Lehman L.H., Celi L.A., Mark R.G.
1. Overview of MIMIC-IV
1.1 What Is MIMIC-IV
  • Publicly available database from Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center (BIDMC)
  • Covers 2008–2019 (decade of data)
  • Contains: patient measurements, orders, diagnoses, procedures, treatments, free-text clinical notes
  • Designed to reduce barriers to conducting clinical research
1.2 Purpose & Goals
  • Support wide array of research studies and educational material
  • Enable analysis of routine clinical practice data
  • Provide contemporary data reflecting current medical practices
1.3 Comparison to Other Critical Care Datasets
  • MIMIC-III (2015): ~40,000 patients --> predecessor to MIMIC-IV
  • eICU-CRD v2.0: 200,859 stays across 208 hospitals in continental US
  • AmsterdamUMCdb: 23,106 admissions at single Dutch academic center
  • HiRID: ~34,000 admissions with high-resolution data (1 entry every 2 minutes)
  • PIC Database: Pediatric patients from China, 12,881 patients, 13,941 ICU stays
1.4 Gaps MIMIC-IV Addresses
  • Contemporary data (2008–2019)
  • New precise digital sources --> electronic medicine administration record
  • Modular organization allowing linkage to external departments and data types
2. Data Acquisition Process
2.1 Primary Sources
  • BIDMC data warehouse --> Microsoft SQL Server Database
  • MetaVision clinical information system (iMDsoft, Israel) --> ICU bedside data
  • Data transferred to MIT via Virtual Private Network (VPN)
2.2 Clinical Cohort Criteria
  • All patients admitted to emergency department or ICU between 2008–2019
  • Excluded: patients <18 years old on first visit
  • Excluded: individuals on enhanced protection list
  • Merged duplicate medical record numbers for same individual
2.3 External Data Sources Integrated
  • ICD codes (versions 9 & 10): ICD-9-CM, ICD-9-PCS, ICD-10-CM, ICD-10-PCS
  • DRG codes: AP-DRG and MS-DRG versions
  • HCPCS billing codes
  • Codes initialized with 2020 version --> added historical codes no longer in current use
2.4 Out-of-Hospital Mortality Linking
  • Did NOT use Social Security Death Master File (SSDMF) --> data quality concerns
  • Used instead: Massachusetts State Registry of Vital Records and Statistics
  • Matching algorithm: name + SSN + date of birth using RecordLinkage library v0.15
  • Used fuzzy matching: Jaro-Winkler for names, Levenshtein for SSN, date-specific edit distance
2.4.1 Matching Results
  • Original patients: 590,325 unique individuals
  • Initial candidates: 717,092 links for 150,832 unique individuals (25.6%)
  • Final linked: 65,805 of 150,832 (43.6%) to state death records
  • Exact match on last name + DOB + SSN: 51,022/65,803 (77.5%)
  • Exact match on last name + DOB + approximate first name: 11,634/65,803 (17.7%)
3. Database Structure & Modules
3.1 Three Core Modules
  • hosp --> hospital-wide data (ADT records, labs, medications, billing)
  • icu --> ICU bedside documentation
  • note --> free-text clinical notes
3.2 Linking Identifiers Across Modules
  • subject_id --> unique patient identifier (assigned randomly for deidentification)
  • hadm_id --> single hospitalization identifier
  • deidentified dates and times
3.3 Modular Design Benefits
  • Enables linkage to external departments
  • Supports different data modalities (imaging, genomics, waveforms)
  • Similar to database schemas for organization
4. Hospital Module (hosp) Details
4.1 Patient Tracking Tables
4.1.1 patients Table
  • subject_id --> unique per patient
  • anchor_year --> deidentified year (e.g., 2150)
  • anchorage --> patient's age at anchoryear
  • anchoryeargroup --> approximate true admission year range (e.g., 2011–2013)
  • dod --> date of death --> censored at 1 year from last hospital discharge
4.1.2 admissions Table
  • Each row = one hospital admission
  • Links to subject_id in patients table
4.1.3 transfers Table
  • Documents patient movement within hospital
  • Used to derive ICU stays
4.2 Administration Tables
  • services --> hospital service patient is under during hospitalization
  • poe, poe_detail --> Provider Order Entry system data
  • Contains: diagnosis orders, imaging orders, consultation orders, treatment orders
4.3 Billing Tables
  • diagnoses_icd --> ICD-9-CM and ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes (max 39 per encounter)
  • seq_num provides approximate ordering but not guaranteed accurate
  • procedures_icd --> ICD-9-PCS and ICD-10-PCS procedure codes
  • drgcodes --> Diagnosis Related Groups (assigned by drg_type column)
  • Used to assign overall hospitalization cost
  • hcpcsevents --> Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System billing events
4.4 Measurement Tables
4.4.1 labevents Table
  • Laboratory measurements from patient specimens
  • specimen_id --> groups multiple measurements on same specimen
  • New columns in MIMIC-IV: storetime, specimenid, refrangelower, refrange_upper, priority, comments
4.4.2 microbiologyevents Table
  • Microbiology culture results (final interpretation only)
  • Hierarchy: specimen --> organism --> isolate --> antibiotic --> dilution
  • Elements repeated for analysis simplification
  • New columns: microspecimenid, testseq, storedate, storetime, testname, quantity, comments
4.4.3 omr Table (Online Medical Record)
  • Data from BIDMC affiliated institutes
  • Contains: blood pressure, height, weight, BMI, eGFR
  • Available from inpatient and outpatient visits
  • Often includes baseline values before hospitalization
4.5 Medication Tables
4.5.1 prescriptions & pharmacy
  • prescriptions --> provider medication orders
  • pharmacy --> detailed compound information
  • Not all prescribed compounds have pharmacy entries
4.5.2 emar & emar_detail (Electronic Medicine Administration Record)
  • Requires barcode scanning of patient wristband + medication at administration
  • Deployed throughout BIDMC between 2014–2016
  • By 2016: all hospital units deployed --> all hospitalizations from 2016 onward have records
  • Records actual administration (not just orders)
  • emarid uniquely identifies rows, emarseq = chronological ordering
4.5.2.1 emar_detail Structure
  • Each formulary dose scanned = one row
  • Primary row (missing parentfieldordinal) --> entire administration event
  • Columns: completedosenotgiven, dosedue
  • Formulary dose rows (increasing parentfieldordinal) --> individual scanned doses
  • Columns: dosegiven, productdescription
5. ICU Module (icu) Details
5.1 Data Source
  • MetaVision clinical information system (iMDsoft, Israel)
  • Only ICU clinical information system used during 2008–2019
5.2 Tables in icu Module
  • icustays --> ICU stay definitions (primary key = stay_id)
  • chartevents --> largest table, bedside documentation
  • datetimeevents --> date-based events (e.g., last dialysis time)
  • inputevents --> continuous and bolus infusions
  • outputevents --> patient outputs (single value at charttime)
  • procedureevents --> processes with start/end times
  • d_items --> dimension table defining itemids
5.3 Star Schema Design
  • All event tables reference d_items for itemid definitions
  • All event tables reference icustays for stay_id
5.4 ICU Stay Definition
  • Derived from transfers table in hosp module
  • Identified using lookup table matching physical location to ICU cost center
  • Consecutive transfers merged into single stay_id
  • intime and outtime available for each ICU stay
  • Transfer to non-ICU ward between stays --> separate stay_id for each
5.5 Event Table Characteristics
  • Each row has: charttime (measurement relevance time), storetime (validation by staff)
  • Contains: subjectid, hadmid, stay_id
  • chartevents --> catch-all for bedside documentation
  • datetimeevents --> date values in value column
6. Notes Module (note) Details
6.1 Discharge Summaries
  • In-depth overview of patient history and hospital course
  • Organized sections:
  • Chief complaint
  • History of present illness
  • Past medical history
  • Brief hospital course
  • Physical exams
  • Discharge diagnoses
6.1.1 Sections Removed During Deidentification
  • Social History section --> removed (high reidentification risk)
  • Discharge Instructions section --> removed
6.2 Radiology Reports
  • Radiologist reports for imaging studies
  • Modalities: x-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound
  • Follows structured reporting best practices
  • Sections: indication, comparison, findings, impression
6.3 Auxiliary Tables (Entity-Attribute-Value Format)
  • discharge_detail --> associated data for discharge summaries
  • radiology_detail --> CPT codes, coded ontology, addendum links
7. Changes from MIMIC-III to MIMIC-IV
7.1 New Tables in MIMIC-IV
  • emar, emar_detail --> electronic medication administration records
  • ingredientevents --> ingredient-level data
  • omr --> online medical record
  • poe, poe_detail --> provider order entry
7.2 Modified Tables
  • labevents: added columns storetime, specimenid, refrange_lower/upper, priority, comments
  • microbiologyevents: added columns microspecimenid, testseq, storedate, storetime, testname, quantity, comments
  • hcpcsevents, dhcpcs: replaced MIMIC-III cptevents and dcpt tables
  • prescriptions:
  • starttime/endtime replaced startdate/enddate
  • Removed: drugnamegeneric, drugnamepoe
  • Added: pharmacyid, formrx
7.3 Renamed Tables
  • inputevents --> equivalent to inputevents_mv in MIMIC-III (CareVue subset removed)
  • procedureevents --> equivalent to procedureevents_mv in MIMIC-III
7.4 Identifier Changes
  • stayid replaced ICU-specific icustayid
  • stay_id is general location-based identifier
  • Used to identify period of stay within a single location
  • All patient identifiers regenerated --> cannot link patients across MIMIC-III and IV
7.5 Backward Compatibility
  • Tables with identical names broadly compatible between versions
  • Enables code and analysis portability
8. Technical Validation Process
8.1 Development Best Practices
  • Interdisciplinary team of scientists and clinicians evaluated throughout development
  • Code reviews conducted
  • Issues tracked using ticket system
  • Version control software used for code management
8.2 Build Process
  • Single, reproducible build script executed all transformations
  • Unit tests created and applied during development
8.3 Integrity Checks
  • Ensures database structure is sound
  • Examples:
  • Every diagnosesicd code present in dicd_diagnoses
  • Every table with patient data has entry in patients table
  • Every table with hadm_id has corresponding entry in admissions
8.4 Consistency Checks
  • Confirms data matches known truth
  • Examples:
  • 99% minimum of hadmid in icustays present in diagnosesicd
  • 99% minimum of ICU stays have at least one heart rate measurement in chartevents
  • Subset comparison against MIMIC-III v1.4 values
8.5 Deidentification Checks
  • Confirms deidentification pipeline successful application
  • Examples:
  • All unique column values present in manually curated allow lists
  • Dates perturbed correctly
  • Sensitive attributes (e.g., race) sufficiently aggregated
9. Access Requirements & Usage
9.1 Access Process
  • Complete training in human research (e.g., CITI training)
  • Sign Data Use Agreement (DUA)
  • DUA requirements:
  • Adequately safeguard dataset
  • Do not attempt reidentification
  • Do not share data with others
  • Report deidentification issues
9.2 Data Access Locations
  • hosp and icu modules: MIMIC-IV project on PhysioNet
  • note module: MIMIC-IV-Note: Deidentified free-text clinical notes project on PhysioNet
9.3 MIMIC Code Repository
  • Enables researchers to share and reuse code
  • Contains SQL scripts for extracting meaningful subsets:
  • Blood gases
  • Ventilation settings
  • Vasopressor treatment
  • Scripts available for: PostgreSQL, MySQL, Google BigQuery
9.4 Derived Resources
  • MIMIC-IV and code-generated data available on cloud for direct interrogation
  • Example analyses and tutorials as executable documents (Jupyter Notebooks, RMarkdown)
  • Source code for published studies using MIMIC-IV available as templates
9.5 CareVue Subset
  • MIMIC-III Clinical Database CareVue subset published for research spanning 2002–2008
  • Contains only MIMIC-III patients not in MIMIC-IV
  • Enables continuity across datasets
10. Demographics Summary (ICU Patients)
Metric Value
Hospital admissions 431,231
ICU admissions 73,181
Unique patients (hospital) 180,733
Unique patients (ICU) 50,920
Age mean (SD) 58.8 (19.2) --> ICU: 64.7 (16.9)
Female gender 224,990 (52.2%) --> ICU: 32,363 (44.2%)
Mean hospital LOS 4.5 days --> ICU: 11.0 days
In-hospital mortality 8,974 (2.1%) --> ICU: 8,519 (11.6%)
One-year mortality 106,218 (24.6%) --> ICU: 28,274 (38.6%)
10.1 Insurance Distribution
  • Medicaid: 41,330 (9.6%) --> ICU: 5,528 (7.6%)
  • Medicare: 160,560 (37.2%) --> ICU: 33,091 (45.2%)
  • Other: 229,341 (53.2%) --> ICU: 34,562 (47.2%)
11. Code & Documentation Availability
11.1 Deidentification Methods
  • Rule-based approach: published and publicly available (Douglass et al.)
  • Neural network: publicly available, described elsewhere (Johnson et al. 2020)
  • Combined approach used for free-text and structured data comment fields
  • PHI replaced with three consecutive underscores ("_")
11.2 Documentation
  • Online documentation at: https://mimic.mit.edu
  • Source code for documentation open for contributions
11.3 Build Process Limitations
  • Full build process cannot be publicly released (contains sensitive patient information)
  • Deidentification algorithms published separately
12. Key Statistics Summary
Statistic Value
Data collection period 2008–2019 (11 years)
Total hospital admissions 431,231
Unique hospital patients 180,733
ICU admissions 73,181
Unique ICU patients 50,920
Mean hospital LOS 4.5 days (SD: 6.6)
Mean ICU LOS 11.0 days (SD: 13.3)
In-hospital mortality 2.1% hospital, 11.6% ICU
One-year mortality 24.6% hospital, 38.6% ICU
13. HIPAA Safe Harbor Deidentification
13.1 Method Applied
  • Custom algorithms developed to find and remove 18 HIPAA identifiers
  • Look-up tables used for random ID assignment
  • Dates perturbed using patient-level offset
  • Preserves interval between time points for each patient
13.2 Free-Text Deidentification
  • Combined two published algorithms (Neamatullah et al., Johnson et al.)
  • If either algorithm identified PHI --> removed and replaced with "_"
  • Applied to:
  • Free-text clinical notes in note module
  • Free-text elements in structured data (e.g., comment fields)
13.3 Validation
  • Thorough human review conducted
  • Created exhaustive allow lists filtering data prior to release
